name: Pho Bo (Beef Pho)
description: Aromatic Vietnamese beef noodle soup with rice noodles, thinly sliced beef, and fragrant star anise broth
instructions: Char onion and ginger under a broiler until blackened on all sides, about 10 minutes.
Blanch beef bones in boiling water for 5 minutes, drain, and rinse clean.
Simmer bones with charred onion, ginger, star anise, cinnamon, and 2.5 liters of water for 60 minutes, skimming impurities.
Season broth with fish sauce and a pinch of sugar, strain through a fine sieve.
Cook rice noodles according to package directions, drain and divide among bowls.
Slice beef sirloin paper-thin against the grain.
Ladle boiling broth over noodles and raw beef slices — the hot broth cooks the beef instantly.
Serve with bean sprouts, Thai basil, lime wedges, and hoisin sauce on the side.
tips: Freeze beef for 20 minutes before slicing for paper-thin cuts
Toast spices in a dry pan for deeper flavor

name: Pho Ga (Chicken Pho)
description: Light and fragrant Vietnamese chicken noodle soup with tender poached chicken and fresh herbs
instructions: Place whole chicken in a large pot, cover with cold water, and bring to a boil.
Skim foam, then add charred onion, ginger, and coriander seeds. Simmer for 35 minutes.
Remove chicken and let cool. Shred the meat and discard bones.
Strain broth and season with fish sauce and sugar.
Cook rice noodles, drain, and divide among bowls.
Top noodles with shredded chicken, ladle hot broth over.
Garnish with bean sprouts, cilantro, green onion, and lime wedges.
tips: Do not boil the chicken vigorously — gentle simmering keeps the broth clear

name: Bun Bo Hue (Spicy Beef Noodle Soup)
description: Bold and spicy noodle soup from Hue with lemongrass-infused broth, beef shank, and thick round noodles
instructions: Blanch beef shank and pork bones, rinse, and return to a clean pot with fresh water.
Bruise lemongrass stalks and add to the pot. Simmer for 90 minutes until beef is tender.
Heat annatto oil in a small pan, fry chili flakes and shrimp paste until fragrant.
Stir the chili-shrimp paste mixture into the broth. Season with fish sauce.
Remove beef shank and slice into thin rounds.
Cook thick rice noodles, drain, and place in bowls.
Top with sliced beef, ladle broth over, and serve with banana blossom, mint, and lime.
tips: Annatto oil gives the signature red color — find it at Asian grocery stores
Adjust shrimp paste gradually as it is very pungent

name: Bun Cha (Grilled Pork with Noodles)
description: Hanoi-style grilled pork patties and sliced pork belly served with rice vermicelli and tangy dipping broth
instructions: Marinate ground pork and sliced pork belly with garlic, 2 tbsp fish sauce, and 1 tbsp sugar for 30 minutes.
Shape ground pork into small flat patties.
Grill pork patties and pork belly slices over charcoal or high heat until charred and cooked through.
Make dipping broth: combine remaining fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, rice vinegar, and warm water. Stir until sugar dissolves.
Cook rice vermicelli, rinse in cold water, and drain.
Julienne carrot and add to the dipping broth along with the grilled pork.
Serve vermicelli on the side with lettuce, herbs, and the bowl of pork in broth for dipping.
tips: Charcoal grilling gives the authentic smoky flavor that defines this dish

name: Banh Mi Thit (Vietnamese Baguette Sandwich)
description: Crispy Vietnamese baguette stuffed with savory pork, pate, pickled vegetables, and fresh cilantro
instructions: Pickle julienned daikon and carrot in rice vinegar, sugar, and a pinch of salt for 20 minutes.
Season pork loin with soy sauce. Pan-sear or roast until cooked through, about 15 minutes. Slice thinly.
Split baguettes lengthwise without cutting all the way through.
Spread pate on one side and mayonnaise on the other.
Layer sliced pork, pickled vegetables, cucumber slices, cilantro, and jalapeno slices.
Press gently and serve immediately while the bread is still crispy.
tips: Use a light airy baguette — dense bread ruins the texture balance
Quick-pickle the vegetables while the pork cooks to save time

name: Com Tam (Broken Rice with Pork)
description: Saigon-style broken rice topped with grilled marinated pork chop, fried egg, and sweet fish sauce
instructions: Mince lemongrass, garlic, and shallot. Mix with fish sauce, sugar, and oil to make a marinade.
Marinate pork chops for at least 30 minutes, or overnight for best flavor.
Cook broken rice in a rice cooker or pot with slightly less water than regular rice.
Grill or pan-fry pork chops over high heat until charred and cooked through, about 5 minutes per side.
Fry eggs sunny-side up in a little oil.
Make sweet fish sauce (nuoc cham): combine fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, water, and minced chili.
Plate rice with pork chop, fried egg, sliced cucumber, and tomato. Drizzle with nuoc cham.
tips: Broken rice cooks faster than regular rice — reduce water by 10%

name: Goi Cuon (Fresh Spring Rolls)
description: Translucent rice paper rolls filled with shrimp, pork, vermicelli, and fresh herbs with peanut dipping sauce
instructions: Boil pork belly until cooked through, about 20 minutes. Slice thinly.
Cook rice vermicelli, rinse in cold water, and drain.
Halve shrimp lengthwise.
Dip one rice paper wrapper in warm water for 5 seconds until pliable.
Layer lettuce, herbs, vermicelli, pork, and shrimp on the wrapper. Fold sides in and roll tightly.
Make peanut sauce: warm hoisin sauce with peanut butter, garlic, and a splash of water until smooth. Add chili sauce.
Serve rolls with peanut dipping sauce.
tips: Do not over-soak rice paper — it should still be slightly firm when you start rolling

name: Cha Gio (Fried Spring Rolls)
description: Crispy golden Vietnamese fried spring rolls filled with pork, shrimp, taro, and glass noodles
instructions: Soak glass noodles and wood ear mushrooms in warm water for 15 minutes. Drain and chop finely.
Mince shrimp. Grate taro and carrot.
Combine ground pork, shrimp, taro, carrot, noodles, mushrooms, fish sauce, pepper, and egg.
Briefly dip rice paper in water, place filling in the center, fold in sides and roll tightly.
Heat oil to 170°C. Fry spring rolls in batches until golden and crispy, about 5 minutes.
Drain on paper towels and serve with lettuce, herbs, and nuoc cham dipping sauce.
tips: Double-wrap with rice paper for extra crispiness
Fry at moderate heat to cook filling through without burning the wrapper

name: Bo Luc Lac (Shaking Beef)
description: Tender cubes of beef seared in a hot wok with garlic, soy sauce, and served over peppery watercress
instructions: Cut beef into 2cm cubes. Marinate with garlic, soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and black pepper for 15 minutes.
Heat oil in a wok over very high heat until smoking.
Sear beef cubes in a single layer without stirring for 1 minute to get a hard sear.
Shake and toss the wok (hence the name) and cook for another minute for medium-rare.
Remove beef. Quickly toss sliced onion and tomato in the same wok for 30 seconds.
Arrange watercress on a plate, top with beef, onion, and tomato. Squeeze lime juice over.
tips: Do not overcrowd the wok — cook in two batches if needed for proper searing

name: Ca Kho To (Caramelized Fish in Clay Pot)
description: Catfish fillets braised in a sweet and savory caramel sauce with fish sauce and black pepper in a clay pot
instructions: Cut catfish into thick steaks. Season lightly with fish sauce and pepper.
In a clay pot or heavy saucepan, make caramel by melting sugar with a splash of water until amber-colored.
Add sliced shallot and garlic to the caramel and stir for 30 seconds.
Add fish pieces, remaining fish sauce, coconut water, and sliced chili.
Simmer uncovered on low heat for 25 minutes, basting fish occasionally, until sauce is thick and glossy.
Garnish with green onion and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Watch the caramel carefully — it goes from amber to burnt in seconds
Coconut water (not coconut milk) gives authentic sweetness

name: Thit Kho (Caramelized Pork Belly)
description: Meltingly tender pork belly slow-braised in caramel sauce with hard-boiled eggs and coconut water
instructions: Cut pork belly into large cubes. Hard-boil eggs and peel.
Make caramel: heat sugar with a splash of water until deep amber.
Add pork cubes to the caramel and toss to coat evenly.
Add garlic, shallot, fish sauce, coconut water, and black pepper.
Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer covered for 40 minutes.
Add peeled hard-boiled eggs and simmer for another 10 minutes until sauce is thick.
Garnish with green onion and serve over steamed jasmine rice.
tips: This dish tastes even better the next day as flavors deepen overnight

name: Goi Ga (Vietnamese Chicken Salad)
description: Shredded poached chicken tossed with cabbage, Vietnamese coriander, crispy shallots, and tangy lime dressing
instructions: Poach chicken breast in simmering water for 15 minutes. Remove and shred finely.
Thinly slice cabbage, julienne carrot, and thinly slice onion. Soak onion in ice water for 5 minutes to soften bite.
Make dressing: whisk lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, and minced chili until sugar dissolves.
Combine chicken, cabbage, carrot, drained onion, and herbs in a large bowl.
Pour dressing over and toss well.
Top with crushed roasted peanuts and fried shallots. Serve immediately.
tips: Shred chicken along the grain for the characteristic texture

name: Canh Chua (Sour Soup with Fish)
description: Vietnamese tamarind-based sour soup with fish, pineapple, tomatoes, okra, and bean sprouts
instructions: Cut fish into large chunks. Season lightly with fish sauce.
Dissolve tamarind paste in 1 cup of warm water, strain out seeds.
Bring 1.5 liters of water to a boil. Add tamarind water, pineapple chunks, and quartered tomatoes.
Simmer for 5 minutes. Add fish pieces and halved okra.
Cook for 8 minutes until fish is done. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
Add bean sprouts, stir briefly, and remove from heat.
Garnish with rice paddy herb and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Do not stir too vigorously after adding fish — the pieces will break apart

name: Bun Rieu (Crab Noodle Soup)
description: Rich tomato-based noodle soup with crab and pork egg froth, tofu puffs, and fresh herbs
instructions: Mix crab paste with ground pork and beaten eggs to form the crab froth mixture.
Bring 2 liters of water to a boil. Gently pour in the crab mixture — it will float and form a frothy raft. Do not stir.
Add quartered tomatoes and annatto oil. Simmer for 20 minutes.
Season broth with fish sauce and shrimp paste.
Halve tofu puffs and add to the soup.
Cook rice vermicelli, drain, and divide among bowls.
Ladle soup with crab froth and tofu over noodles. Serve with lettuce, herbs, and lime.
tips: Let the crab froth form naturally — stirring it breaks the delicate texture

name: Com Ga (Chicken Rice)
description: Vietnamese chicken rice from Hoi An with turmeric-tinted rice, shredded poached chicken, and onion garnish
instructions: Poach chicken thighs in seasoned water with half an onion for 25 minutes. Reserve the broth.
Shred chicken and toss with a splash of fish sauce and pepper.
Cook rice using chicken broth instead of water, adding turmeric and garlic for golden color and flavor.
Thinly slice remaining onion and soak in lime juice for a quick pickle.
Fluff turmeric rice and mound on plates.
Top with shredded chicken, pickled onion, and Vietnamese coriander. Serve with chili sauce and lime.
tips: Using chicken broth to cook the rice is essential — it absorbs all the flavor

name: Banh Xeo (Vietnamese Crispy Pancake)
description: Crispy turmeric-coconut crepe filled with shrimp, pork, bean sprouts, and served with lettuce wraps
instructions: Make batter: whisk rice flour, coconut milk, turmeric, and 1 cup water until smooth. Let rest 15 minutes.
Slice pork belly thinly. Peel and devein shrimp.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over high heat. Sear a few shrimp and pork slices for 1 minute.
Pour a thin layer of batter over the protein, swirling to coat. Add bean sprouts and sliced onion to one half.
Cover and cook for 3 minutes until edges are crispy and golden.
Fold the pancake in half and slide onto a plate.
Repeat with remaining batter. Serve with lettuce leaves, herbs, and nuoc cham for wrapping.
tips: The key to crispiness is generous oil and high heat — do not skimp

name: Lau (Vietnamese Hot Pot)
description: Communal Vietnamese hot pot with a fragrant lemongrass and tomato broth, assorted seafood, meats, and vegetables
instructions: Prepare broth: simmer lemongrass, quartered tomatoes, and garlic in 2 liters of water for 15 minutes. Season with fish sauce.
Slice beef thinly, clean and score squid, peel shrimp. Arrange on a platter.
Wash and cut napa cabbage, morning glory, and mushrooms. Arrange on another platter.
Bring broth to a rolling boil in a hot pot at the center of the table.
Diners cook ingredients by dipping them into the simmering broth.
Cook rice vermicelli in the broth at the end to soak up all the flavors.
Serve with dipping sauces — satay sauce and nuoc cham.
tips: Keep the broth at a rolling boil throughout the meal for safe cooking

name: Bun Thit Nuong (Grilled Pork Vermicelli)
description: Cool rice vermicelli bowl topped with charcoal-grilled lemongrass pork, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, and nuoc cham
instructions: Slice pork thinly. Marinate with minced lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, sugar, and a little oil for 30 minutes.
Grill pork over high heat or under a broiler until charred and caramelized, about 3 minutes per side.
Cook rice vermicelli, rinse in cold water, and drain.
Make nuoc cham: mix fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, water, garlic, and chili.
Assemble bowls: vermicelli on the bottom, then lettuce, cucumber, herbs, pickled vegetables, and grilled pork on top.
Drizzle generously with nuoc cham, sprinkle crushed peanuts, and serve.
tips: This is meant to be eaten at room temperature — do not reheat the vermicelli

name: Ga Nuong (Vietnamese Grilled Chicken)
description: Whole chicken pieces marinated in lemongrass, honey, and fish sauce then grilled until golden and juicy
instructions: Mince lemongrass and garlic. Combine with honey, fish sauce, lime juice, chili flakes, oil, and pepper.
Score chicken legs deeply and coat thoroughly with marinade. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Preheat grill or oven to 200°C.
Grill chicken over medium heat, turning occasionally, for 30-35 minutes until charred and cooked through.
Baste with remaining marinade during the last 5 minutes of cooking.
Rest for 5 minutes, garnish with green onion, and serve with steamed rice and pickled vegetables.
tips: Score the chicken deeply so the marinade penetrates the meat thoroughly

name: Rau Muong Xao Toi (Stir-Fried Water Spinach)
description: Crunchy water spinach stir-fried with garlic in a blazing hot wok — a quintessential Vietnamese side dish
instructions: Wash water spinach thoroughly. Cut into 8cm lengths, separating stems from leaves.
Mince garlic.
Heat oil in a wok over maximum heat until smoking.
Add garlic and stir for 10 seconds until fragrant.
Add stems first, stir-fry for 30 seconds, then add leaves.
Add fish sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar. Toss vigorously for 1 minute until just wilted but still crunchy.
Serve immediately — this dish does not hold well.
tips: The wok must be screaming hot — lukewarm stir-frying makes it watery and limp

name: Dau Hu Sot Ca Chua (Tofu in Tomato Sauce)
description: Crispy pan-fried tofu simmered in a savory Vietnamese tomato sauce with green onion
instructions: Press tofu to remove excess water. Cut into triangles.
Pan-fry tofu in oil until golden and crispy on both sides. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, sauté minced garlic until fragrant.
Add diced tomato and cook until they break down into a sauce, about 5 minutes.
Season with fish sauce, sugar, and pepper.
Return tofu to the pan and simmer in the tomato sauce for 3 minutes.
Garnish with chopped green onion and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Press tofu for at least 15 minutes for the crispiest result

name: Canh Bi Dao (Winter Melon Soup)
description: Light and soothing Vietnamese soup with tender winter melon, shrimp, and a clear savory broth
instructions: Peel winter melon, remove seeds, and cut into bite-sized cubes.
Peel and devein shrimp. Mince garlic.
Sauté garlic in oil until fragrant. Add shrimp and cook for 1 minute.
Add 1 liter of water and bring to a boil.
Add winter melon cubes and simmer for 15 minutes until translucent and tender.
Season with fish sauce and pepper. Garnish with green onion and serve.
tips: Winter melon is done when it turns translucent — do not overcook or it becomes mushy

name: Bo Kho (Vietnamese Beef Stew)
description: Hearty Vietnamese beef stew braised with lemongrass, star anise, and carrots in a rich tomato-spiced broth
instructions: Cut beef chuck into large cubes. Season with fish sauce and five-spice powder.
Heat annatto oil in a heavy pot. Sear beef in batches until browned on all sides.
Sauté garlic, onion, and bruised lemongrass until fragrant.
Add tomato paste, star anise, and cinnamon. Cook for 1 minute.
Return beef to pot. Add coconut water and enough water to cover. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer covered for 60 minutes. Add chunked carrots and simmer 20 more minutes.
Adjust seasoning with fish sauce. Serve with crusty bread or rice noodles, garnished with Thai basil.
tips: Bo kho is traditionally eaten with a crusty Vietnamese baguette for dipping

name: Mi Quang (Turmeric Noodles)
description: Central Vietnamese turmeric-tinted noodles with shrimp, pork, peanuts, and a small amount of rich broth
instructions: Slice pork thinly and marinate with turmeric, fish sauce, and garlic.
Sauté pork in annatto oil until cooked. Add shrimp and cook until pink.
Add water and sliced onion. Simmer for 10 minutes to make a concentrated broth.
Cook wide rice noodles and drain.
Place noodles in bowls. Top with pork and shrimp.
Ladle a small amount of broth (this dish is not soupy). Top with crushed peanuts, sesame rice crackers, lettuce, and banana blossom.
tips: Mi Quang uses very little broth compared to pho — just enough to moisten the noodles

name: Hu Tieu Nam Vang (Phnom Penh Noodle Soup)
description: Southern Vietnamese clear pork broth noodle soup with shrimp, pork, and liver, topped with fried garlic
instructions: Simmer pork bones and dried shrimp in water for 40 minutes to make a clear broth. Skim impurities.
Season broth with fish sauce and rock sugar.
Fry sliced garlic in oil until golden and crispy. Set aside.
Form ground pork into small balls. Poach in the broth until cooked.
Cook hu tieu noodles and drain into bowls.
Top with shrimp, pork balls, bean sprouts, and Chinese chives.
Ladle broth over and garnish with fried garlic.
tips: Dried shrimp in the broth is what gives hu tieu its distinctive sweetness

name: Cha Ca La Vong (Turmeric Fish with Dill)
description: Hanoi's iconic dish of turmeric-marinated fish pan-fried with dill and green onion, served with rice noodles
instructions: Cut fish into large chunks. Marinate with turmeric, galangal, shrimp paste, and fish sauce for 20 minutes.
Cook rice vermicelli and drain.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium-high heat.
Pan-fry fish pieces until golden on both sides, about 3 minutes per side.
Add generous amounts of fresh dill and green onion to the pan. Toss gently with the fish for 1 minute.
Serve fish and dill over vermicelli, topped with crushed peanuts and nuoc cham on the side.
tips: Use a generous amount of dill — it is the star of this dish, not just a garnish

name: Com Chien (Vietnamese Fried Rice)
description: Simple Vietnamese fried rice with egg, lap xuong sausage, vegetables, and fish sauce seasoning
instructions: Dice Chinese sausage and carrot. Mince garlic. Chop green onion.
Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Scramble eggs quickly and set aside.
Stir-fry sausage and carrot for 2 minutes until sausage renders its fat.
Add garlic and peas. Stir for 30 seconds.
Add cold rice and break up clumps. Toss over high heat for 3 minutes.
Add fish sauce, white pepper, and scrambled egg. Toss to combine.
Garnish with green onion and serve.
tips: Day-old cold rice is essential — freshly cooked rice will turn mushy

name: Ga Kho Gung (Ginger Chicken)
description: Chicken pieces braised in a sweet-savory ginger and caramel sauce — a comforting everyday Vietnamese dish
instructions: Cut chicken into pieces. Julienne ginger, mince garlic and shallot.
Make caramel: heat sugar with water until amber in a clay pot or heavy pan.
Add chicken pieces and toss in the caramel to coat.
Add ginger, garlic, shallot, fish sauce, and pepper. Stir well.
Add just enough water to partially cover. Bring to a boil.
Simmer covered for 25 minutes until chicken is tender and sauce is thick.
Garnish with green onion and sliced chili. Serve with steamed rice.
tips: Use young ginger for a milder, less fibrous result

name: Canh Rau Mong Toi (Malabar Spinach Soup)
description: Simple and nutritious Vietnamese soup with Malabar spinach and ground pork in a clear broth
instructions: Wash Malabar spinach and pick leaves from stems.
Season ground pork with a splash of fish sauce and pepper.
Sauté garlic in oil until fragrant. Add ground pork and cook, breaking it up, for 2 minutes.
Add 1 liter of water and bring to a boil.
Add Malabar spinach leaves. Cook for 2 minutes until just wilted.
Season with remaining fish sauce and pepper. Serve hot with rice.
tips: Malabar spinach has a slightly mucilaginous texture — this is normal and adds body to the soup

name: Suon Nuong (Grilled Pork Chops)
description: Vietnamese-style grilled pork chops marinated in lemongrass, garlic, and honey served over rice
instructions: Mince lemongrass, garlic, and shallot. Mix with fish sauce, honey, pepper, and oil.
Pound pork chops lightly to even thickness. Coat with marinade and refrigerate for 1 hour.
Cook jasmine rice.
Grill pork chops over medium-high heat for 5 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Let rest for 3 minutes before serving.
Serve over rice with pickled vegetables and nuoc cham.
tips: Pound the chops to even thickness so they cook uniformly on the grill

name: Bap Xao (Stir-Fried Corn with Dried Shrimp)
description: Sweet corn kernels stir-fried with butter, dried shrimp, and green onion — a popular Vietnamese street food
instructions: Soak dried shrimp in warm water for 10 minutes. Drain and chop roughly.
Melt butter in a wok over high heat.
Add garlic and dried shrimp. Stir-fry for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add corn kernels and toss over high heat for 3 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and sugar. Stir for another minute.
Add chopped green onion, toss briefly, and serve immediately.
tips: Fresh corn is best, but frozen works in a pinch — just thaw and drain well first

name: Nom Du Du (Green Papaya Salad)
description: Crunchy Vietnamese green papaya salad with beef jerky, herbs, and a sweet-sour-spicy lime dressing
instructions: Peel and julienne green papaya and carrot. Soak in ice water for 10 minutes for crispness.
Shred or tear beef jerky into thin strips.
Make dressing: whisk lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, and minced chili until sugar dissolves.
Drain papaya and carrot well. Toss with dressing, herbs, and beef jerky.
Top with crushed peanuts and fried shallots. Serve immediately.
tips: Soaking the julienned papaya in ice water is the secret to maximum crunch

name: Ca Ri Ga (Vietnamese Chicken Curry)
description: Mild and coconut-rich Vietnamese chicken curry with sweet potato, lemongrass, and warm spices
instructions: Cut chicken into large pieces. Peel and cube sweet potato.
Sauté onion, garlic, and bruised lemongrass in oil until fragrant.
Add curry powder and stir for 30 seconds until aromatic.
Add chicken and sear for 3 minutes until lightly browned.
Pour in coconut milk and add sweet potato. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
Simmer for 30 minutes until chicken and sweet potato are tender.
Garnish with Thai basil. Serve with crusty bread or rice.
tips: Vietnamese curry is intentionally milder than Thai curry — do not add extra chili

name: Chao (Vietnamese Rice Porridge)
description: Silky Vietnamese rice porridge topped with shredded chicken, ginger, fried shallots, and fresh herbs
instructions: Bring 2 liters of water to a boil with sliced ginger. Add chicken thighs and simmer for 20 minutes.
Remove chicken, shred, and set aside. Keep the broth simmering.
Add rinsed rice to the broth. Cook for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until thick and porridge-like.
Season with fish sauce and pepper.
Ladle porridge into bowls. Top with shredded chicken.
Garnish with fried shallots, green onion, cilantro, and a squeeze of lime.
tips: For silkier porridge, soak rice for 30 minutes before cooking

name: Dau Phu Chien Sa (Lemongrass Tofu)
description: Crispy fried tofu tossed in a fragrant lemongrass and chili sauce — a beloved Vietnamese vegetarian dish
instructions: Press tofu and cut into cubes. Mince lemongrass, garlic, and chili.
Shallow-fry tofu in oil until golden and crispy on all sides. Remove and drain.
In the same pan with 1 tbsp oil, stir-fry lemongrass, garlic, and chili for 1 minute.
Add soy sauce, sugar, and a splash of water. Stir to make a sauce.
Return crispy tofu to the pan and toss to coat evenly in the lemongrass sauce.
Garnish with green onion and pepper. Serve with steamed rice.
tips: Finely mince the lemongrass — large pieces are too fibrous to eat

name: Bun Mam (Fermented Fish Noodle Soup)
description: Intensely flavorful Mekong Delta noodle soup with fermented fish broth, pork, shrimp, and eggplant
instructions: Dissolve fermented fish paste in 2 liters of water. Strain to remove solids.
Simmer the broth with bruised lemongrass and pineapple for 20 minutes.
Boil pork belly until cooked, about 20 minutes. Slice thinly.
Add sugar and adjust fish sauce to balance the broth.
Slice eggplant into rounds and add to the broth. Cook for 5 minutes.
Add shrimp and cook until pink, about 3 minutes.
Cook vermicelli, drain into bowls. Ladle broth with pork, shrimp, and eggplant over. Serve with bean sprouts and basil.
tips: The smell of fermented fish paste is pungent raw but mellows into deep umami when cooked

name: Classic Meatloaf
description: Traditional American meatloaf with ground beef, breadcrumbs, and a sweet tangy ketchup glaze
instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C (350°F). Finely dice onion and mince garlic.
Combine ground beef, breadcrumbs, eggs, milk, diced onion, garlic, 2 tbsp ketchup, Worcestershire sauce, salt, and pepper.
Mix gently until just combined — do not overwork the meat.
Shape into a loaf on a lined baking sheet or press into a loaf pan.
Mix remaining ketchup with brown sugar for the glaze. Spread over the top.
Bake for 55-60 minutes until internal temperature reaches 70°C (160°F).
Rest for 10 minutes before slicing. Serve with mashed potatoes and green beans.
tips: Do not compact the meat too tightly — a gentle hand makes a more tender meatloaf

name: BBQ Pulled Pork
description: Slow-cooked pork shoulder shredded and tossed in smoky barbecue sauce, served on soft buns
instructions: Mix smoked paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, cumin, brown sugar, salt, and pepper. Rub all over the pork shoulder.
Place pork in a slow cooker or Dutch oven with apple cider vinegar.
Cook on low for 3-4 hours (slow cooker) or braise in a 150°C oven covered for 3 hours until fork-tender.
Shred the pork with two forks, discarding excess fat.
Toss shredded pork with BBQ sauce and return to heat for 15 minutes.
Toast buns lightly. Pile pulled pork on buns and top with coleslaw.
tips: Low and slow is the only way — do not rush the cooking or the pork will be tough

name: New England Clam Chowder
description: Thick and creamy New England-style clam chowder with tender potatoes, salt pork, and fresh clams
instructions: Dice salt pork and render in a heavy pot until crispy. Remove cracklings and set aside.
Sauté diced onion and celery in the rendered fat and butter until softened.
Sprinkle flour over vegetables and stir for 2 minutes to make a roux.
Add diced potatoes, clam juice, bay leaf, and thyme. Simmer for 15 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Add chopped clams, cream, and milk. Heat gently for 5 minutes — do not boil.
Season with salt and white pepper. Serve topped with reserved pork cracklings and oyster crackers.
tips: Never boil the chowder after adding cream — it will curdle

name: Southern Fried Chicken
description: Crispy double-dipped Southern fried chicken with a seasoned flour coating and buttermilk brine
instructions: Soak chicken pieces in buttermilk with salt for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight.
Mix flour with paprika, garlic powder, cayenne, salt, and pepper.
Remove chicken from buttermilk. Dredge in seasoned flour, dip back in buttermilk, then dredge again.
Heat oil to 170°C (340°F) in a deep heavy pot.
Fry chicken in batches for 12-15 minutes, turning once, until golden and internal temperature reaches 75°C.
Drain on a wire rack for 5 minutes. Serve with coleslaw and biscuits.
tips: The double dredge is the secret to the thick, craggy crust

name: Jambalaya
description: Spicy Cajun one-pot rice dish with andouille sausage, chicken, shrimp, and the holy trinity of vegetables
instructions: Season chicken with Cajun seasoning. Sear in a large pot until browned. Remove and set aside.
Sauté sliced andouille until browned. Remove.
Cook the holy trinity (diced onion, celery, bell pepper) and garlic in the drippings for 5 minutes.
Add tomatoes, chicken broth, and rice. Return chicken and sausage.
Bring to a boil, cover, reduce heat, and simmer for 20 minutes until rice is cooked.
Add shrimp in the last 5 minutes. Cover and cook until shrimp are pink.
Fluff and serve garnished with chopped green onion.
tips: Do not stir the rice while it simmers — let it cook undisturbed for the best texture

name: Chicken Pot Pie
description: Comforting American chicken pot pie with tender chicken, vegetables, and creamy sauce under a flaky golden crust
instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Dice potato and boil until just tender, about 8 minutes.
Melt butter in a large pan. Sauté diced onion until soft.
Stir in flour and cook for 2 minutes to make a roux.
Gradually add chicken broth and milk, stirring constantly until thick and smooth.
Add diced chicken, cooked potato, peas and carrots, and thyme. Season with salt and pepper.
Pour filling into a baking dish. Top with puff pastry, pressing edges to seal. Cut slits for steam. Brush with beaten egg.
Bake for 25-30 minutes until pastry is golden and filling bubbles.
tips: Let the pot pie rest for 10 minutes before serving — the filling will thicken as it cools slightly

name: Cobb Salad
description: Iconic American composed salad with rows of chicken, bacon, avocado, egg, blue cheese, and tomato
instructions: Season and grill or pan-sear chicken breast until cooked through. Slice.
Cook bacon until crispy. Chop into pieces.
Chop romaine lettuce and spread on a large platter.
Arrange rows of sliced chicken, bacon, diced avocado, halved tomatoes, chopped egg, and crumbled blue cheese over the lettuce.
Whisk vinegar, olive oil, mustard, salt, and pepper for the dressing.
Drizzle dressing over the salad or serve on the side. Garnish with chives.
tips: The composed rows are the signature presentation — do not toss the salad

name: Philly Cheesesteak
description: Thinly sliced ribeye steak with melted provolone, sauteed onions, and peppers on a hoagie roll
instructions: Freeze ribeye for 30 minutes, then slice paper-thin against the grain.
Heat oil on a flat griddle or large skillet over high heat.
Sauté sliced onion, pepper, and mushrooms until softened. Set aside.
Spread steak slices on the hot griddle. Season with salt and pepper. Chop and flip as it cooks.
Lay provolone slices over the steak and let it melt.
Split hoagie rolls and fill with cheesy steak and sauteed vegetables. Serve immediately.
tips: A flat griddle gives the most authentic result — the steak should sizzle hard when it hits the surface

name: Mac and Cheese
description: Creamy baked American macaroni and cheese with a sharp cheddar sauce and golden breadcrumb topping
instructions: Preheat oven to 190°C (375°F). Cook macaroni until al dente and drain.
Melt butter in a saucepan. Whisk in flour and cook for 2 minutes.
Gradually add milk, whisking constantly, until thick and smooth.
Remove from heat. Stir in grated cheddar (reserve a handful), mustard, paprika, garlic powder, and salt.
Fold in cooked macaroni and pour into a greased baking dish.
Top with remaining cheese and breadcrumbs tossed with melted butter.
Bake for 20 minutes until golden and bubbling.
tips: Use a mix of sharp and mild cheddar for the best balance of flavor and meltability

name: Cajun Blackened Catfish
description: Catfish fillets coated in bold Cajun spices and seared in a cast-iron skillet until blackened and flaky
instructions: Mix Cajun seasoning, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, and cayenne.
Pat catfish fillets dry and coat generously with the spice mixture on both sides.
Heat a cast-iron skillet over very high heat until smoking.
Add butter and immediately place fillets in the pan. Cook for 3 minutes per side until a dark crust forms.
Squeeze lemon juice over the fish.
Serve over rice, garnished with parsley and lemon wedges.
tips: Open a window — blackening creates dramatic smoke, which is exactly what you want

name: Texas Chili Con Carne
description: Thick and hearty Texas-style chili with chunks of beef, dried chiles, and no beans — as tradition demands
instructions: Toast dried ancho chiles in a dry pan, then soak in hot water for 15 minutes. Blend into a smooth paste.
Cut beef chuck into small cubes (not ground). Season with salt, cumin, and oregano.
Brown beef in oil in batches in a heavy pot. Remove and set aside.
Sauté diced onion and garlic in the same pot until softened.
Add chile paste, tomato paste, and cayenne. Cook for 2 minutes.
Return beef to pot. Add broth. Bring to a boil, then simmer covered for 90 minutes until beef is tender.
Adjust seasoning. Serve with cornbread, sour cream, and shredded cheese.
tips: Real Texas chili uses cubed beef, not ground — and absolutely no beans

name: Shrimp and Grits
description: Creamy Southern stone-ground grits topped with sauteed shrimp, bacon, and a savory pan sauce
instructions: Cook grits in milk and 2 cups water, stirring frequently, for 20 minutes until creamy. Stir in cheese and 1 tbsp butter.
Cook bacon until crispy. Chop and set aside. Reserve the drippings.
Season shrimp with Cajun seasoning. Sauté in bacon drippings for 2 minutes per side until pink. Remove.
Add garlic to the pan, cook 30 seconds. Deglaze with chicken broth and lemon juice.
Return shrimp to the pan and toss in the sauce.
Spoon creamy grits into bowls. Top with shrimp and pan sauce, chopped bacon, and green onion.
tips: Stone-ground grits take longer but have far better texture and flavor than instant

name: Buffalo Chicken Wings
description: Crispy fried chicken wings tossed in spicy cayenne-butter Buffalo sauce, served with celery and blue cheese dip
instructions: Pat chicken wings completely dry. Season with salt and garlic powder.
Heat oil to 190°C (375°F) in a deep pot. Fry wings in batches for 10-12 minutes until golden and crispy.
Drain on a wire rack.
Melt butter and whisk in hot sauce to make Buffalo sauce.
Toss fried wings in Buffalo sauce until evenly coated.
Make blue cheese dip: crumble blue cheese into sour cream and mayonnaise, mix well.
Serve wings with celery sticks and blue cheese dip.
tips: Drying the wings thoroughly before frying is the single most important step for crispiness

name: BLT Club Sandwich
description: Triple-decker club sandwich stacked with crispy bacon, fresh lettuce, tomato, turkey, and mayonnaise
instructions: Cook bacon until very crispy. Drain on paper towels.
Toast all bread slices until golden.
Spread mayonnaise on one side of each toast.
First layer: toast, lettuce, tomato slices, bacon.
Second layer: toast, sliced turkey, avocado slices, more bacon.
Top with third toast. Secure with toothpicks, cut diagonally, and serve with chips or a pickle.
tips: Use thick-cut bacon and cook it very crispy so it does not pull out of the sandwich when bitten

name: Cornbread with Chili
description: Sweet golden Southern cornbread baked in a cast-iron skillet, served alongside a rich bean chili
instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Place a cast-iron skillet with 2 tbsp butter in the oven.
Mix cornmeal, flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt. Whisk in egg, buttermilk, and 2 tbsp melted butter.
Pour batter into the hot skillet. Bake for 20-25 minutes until golden.
Meanwhile, brown ground beef and diced onion. Add chili powder, diced tomatoes, and drained beans.
Simmer chili for 20 minutes until thick. Season to taste.
Slice warm cornbread and serve alongside the chili with sour cream and cheddar.
tips: The sizzling hot skillet creates the coveted crispy bottom crust on the cornbread

name: Gumbo
description: Rich Louisiana gumbo with a dark roux, andouille sausage, chicken, okra, and the holy trinity over rice
instructions: Make a dark roux: whisk oil and flour in a heavy pot over medium heat, stirring constantly for 30-40 minutes until chocolate-brown.
Add the holy trinity (diced onion, celery, bell pepper) and garlic. Cook for 5 minutes.
Gradually stir in chicken broth. Add seasoned chicken pieces and sliced sausage.
Bring to a boil, then simmer for 45 minutes until chicken is tender.
Remove chicken, shred, and return to the pot. Add sliced okra.
Simmer for 15 more minutes. Season with Cajun seasoning and file powder.
Serve over steamed white rice.
tips: A dark roux is the soul of gumbo — do not rush it, and stir constantly to prevent burning

name: Pot Roast with Root Vegetables
description: Fork-tender braised beef chuck roast with carrots, potatoes, and onions in a rich gravy
instructions: Preheat oven to 160°C (325°F). Season the roast generously with salt and pepper.
Sear the roast in oil in a Dutch oven on all sides until deeply browned. Remove.
Sauté quartered onion, garlic, and celery for 3 minutes. Stir in tomato paste.
Deglaze with beef broth, scraping up browned bits. Add thyme.
Return roast to the pot. Cover and braise in the oven for 2 hours.
Add chunked carrots and potatoes around the roast. Cover and cook for 45 more minutes.
Slice roast against the grain. Serve with vegetables and spoon pan gravy over everything.
tips: A proper hard sear on the roast builds the flavor foundation for the entire dish

name: Baked Salmon with Lemon Dill
description: Oven-baked salmon fillets with a bright lemon-dill butter sauce, tender and flaky
instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.
Place salmon fillets on the sheet. Season with salt, pepper, and a brush of olive oil.
Melt butter with minced garlic, lemon juice, Dijon mustard, and chopped dill.
Spoon the lemon-dill butter over each fillet. Lay lemon slices on top.
Arrange trimmed asparagus around the salmon. Drizzle with olive oil.
Bake for 12-15 minutes until salmon flakes easily with a fork.
Serve immediately with remaining pan juices spooned over.
tips: Do not overcook — salmon should be slightly translucent in the very center for the best texture
